column5: {\em a + tyakta} (Bollée 1994a: 207; if this etymology is correct, {\em tyakta} may here have the active meaning of ‘having left out, omitted;not having done', viz., cooking especially for a monk or nun. The {\em locus classicus} may be  Dasav 5,1,17 a-ciyattaṁ na pavise, ciyattaṁ pavise kulaṁ ‘he shouldnot go to a family that did cook especially for him’ -- food which he wasnot allowed to accept and for which reason he could consider those people “unkind” to him --, ‘but he should go where one had abstaïned therefrom’ so as to enable him to take what was offered as being pure. Haribhadra comments this passage by {\em a-prīti-kulaṁ yatra praviśadbhiḥ sādhubhir a-prītir utpadyate na ca nivārayanti kutaścin nimittântarāt. Etad api na praviśet tat-saṁklésa-nimittatva-prasaṅgāt. Ciyattam [...] praviśet kulaṁ tad-anugraha-prasaṅgāt}. Perhaps a-ciyatta, too, should thus be listed among the negative expressions of the {\em a-hiṁsā} type. Charpentier's difficulty [1922: 320] is partly due to his wrong reading a-vyatta at Utt 11, 9. As ciyatta is rare compared to its negative one may assume that many people tried to improve their {\em karman} by meansnot sanctioned by the monastic rules)

{\em a + sati}  loc. sg. masc. of {\em sat}  ‘in the absence of’ (c. gen.). Cf. Schubring 1963: 236; Bollée 1994a: 132; frequently as a-saī ya (often written as -e) for which expletive addition cf. chac ca\ ? Thus, e.g., 1464, 1620, 1726, 2332, 3193 etc. Where the metre requires a-saīe I can only assume analogy or case contamination . PSM assumes a feminine {\em deśī} noun asaï related to {\em asattva} and  explained by a-bhāva. I have left a-saïe where a syllable is anceps.   Adelheid Mette in a p.c. proposes to consider it a loc. of a-saï ~ {\em a-sak̥rt} used as an adjective
